    Getting started¶

    overviewpy makes it easy to get an overview of a dataset by displaying relevant sample information. This notebook walks through all currently implemented functions:

    Function What it does
    overview_tab Tabular overview — one row per id with collapsed time ranges
    overview_na Bar chart of missing-value counts or percentages (column- or row-wise)
    overview_summary Per-column summary: non-null count, unique count, sample values
    overview_plot Connected dot-plot showing id × time coverage
    overview_overlap Bar chart or Venn diagram comparing ID coverage of two data frames
    overview_heat Heat map of observation counts (or percentages) per id-time combination
    overview_crossplot Scatter plot of two conditions with threshold lines dividing the plot into four quadrants
    overview_crosstab 2×2 cross table sorting observations by two conditions and thresholds
    overview_latex LaTeX table from overview_tab or overview_crosstab output
    overview_markdown Markdown table from overview_tab output

    All are available as methods on the Overview class, which is the recommended interface.

    Import libraries¶

    We import the Overview class from overviewpy, along with pandas for data manipulation and numpy to produce missing values in our example data.

    In [1]:
    Copied! 
    from overviewpy.overview import Overview
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
    from overviewpy.overview import Overview import pandas as pd import numpy as np

    Generate data¶

    In the first step, we will generate some data that we will use in the next steps.

    In [2]:
    Copied! 
    # Generate full data

data = {
        'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', 'RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'FRA',\
            'FRA', 'BEL', 'BEL', 'ARG'],
        'year': [2022, 2023, 2021, 2023, 2020, 2019, 2015,\
            2014, 2013, 2002]
    }

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

df.head()
    # Generate full data data = { 'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', 'RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'FRA',\ 'FRA', 'BEL', 'BEL', 'ARG'], 'year': [2022, 2023, 2021, 2023, 2020, 2019, 2015,\ 2014, 2013, 2002] } df = pd.DataFrame(data) df.head()
    Out[2]:
    id year
    0 RWA 2022
    1 RWA 2023
    2 RWA 2021
    3 GAB 2023
    4 GAB 2020

    We also generate a second data frame that contains missing values (np.nan) in both the id and year columns. This dataset is used to demonstrate overview_na and the NA-handling behaviour of overview_tab.

    In [3]:
    Copied! 
    # Generate data with missing values

data_na = {
        'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', 'RWA', np.nan, 'GAB', 'GAB',\
            'FRA', 'FRA', 'BEL', 'BEL', 'ARG', np.nan,  np.nan],
        'year': [2022, 2001, 2000, 2023, 2021, 2023, 2020,\
            2019,  np.nan, 2015, 2014, 2013, 2002]
    }

df_na = pd.DataFrame(data_na)

df_na.head()
    # Generate data with missing values data_na = { 'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', 'RWA', np.nan, 'GAB', 'GAB',\ 'FRA', 'FRA', 'BEL', 'BEL', 'ARG', np.nan, np.nan], 'year': [2022, 2001, 2000, 2023, 2021, 2023, 2020,\ 2019, np.nan, 2015, 2014, 2013, 2002] } df_na = pd.DataFrame(data_na) df_na.head()
    Out[3]:
    id year
    0 RWA 2022.0
    1 RWA 2001.0
    2 RWA 2000.0
    3 NaN 2023.0
    4 GAB 2021.0

    Get an overview of the time distribution in your data¶

    Generate some general overview of the data set using the time and scope conditions with overview_tab. The resulting data frame collapses the time condition for each id by taking into account potential gaps in the time frame.

    Consecutive years are compressed into a range (e.g. 2021-2023); non-consecutive years appear as a comma-separated list (e.g. 2015, 2019).

    In [4]:
    Copied! 
    overview = Overview(df=df, id_col='id', time='year')
df_overview = overview.overview_tab()

print(df_overview)
    overview = Overview(df=df, id_col='id', time='year') df_overview = overview.overview_tab() print(df_overview)
        id  time_frame
9  ARG        2002
7  BEL   2013-2014
5  FRA  2015, 2019
3  GAB  2020, 2023
0  RWA   2021-2023

    Missing values in id or time are automatically dropped and a UserWarning is raised for each affected variable. The code below captures those warnings explicitly so you can inspect them:

    In [5]:
    Copied! 
    import warnings

data_with_na = {
    'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', np.nan, 'GAB'],
    'year': [2022, np.nan, 2021, 2020],
}
df_with_na = pd.DataFrame(data_with_na)

with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as caught:
    warnings.simplefilter('always')
    overview_with_na = Overview(df=df_with_na, id_col='id', time='year')
    df_overview_na = overview_with_na.overview_tab()

for w in caught:
    print(w.message)

print(df_overview_na)
    import warnings data_with_na = { 'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', np.nan, 'GAB'], 'year': [2022, np.nan, 2021, 2020], } df_with_na = pd.DataFrame(data_with_na) with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as caught: warnings.simplefilter('always') overview_with_na = Overview(df=df_with_na, id_col='id', time='year') df_overview_na = overview_with_na.overview_tab() for w in caught: print(w.message) print(df_overview_na)
    There is at least one missing value in your id variable. The missing value is automatically deleted.
There is at least one missing value in your time variable. The missing value is automatically deleted.
    id time_frame
3  GAB     2020.0
0  RWA     2022.0

    Export an overview table as Markdown¶

    overview_markdown converts the result of overview_tab into a Markdown table. The output can be printed, copy-pasted into any Markdown document, or saved directly as a .md file.

    In [6]:
    Copied! 
    print(overview.overview_markdown())
    print(overview.overview_markdown())
    ## Time and scope of the sample

| Sample | Time frame |
|---|---|
| ARG | 2002 |
| BEL | 2013-2014 |
| FRA | 2015, 2019 |
| GAB | 2020, 2023 |
| RWA | 2021-2023 |
    In [7]:
    Copied! 
    print(overview.overview_markdown(
    title='My sample',
    id_label='Country',
    time_label='Years covered',
))
    print(overview.overview_markdown( title='My sample', id_label='Country', time_label='Years covered', ))
    ## My sample

| Country | Years covered |
|---|---|
| ARG | 2002 |
| BEL | 2013-2014 |
| FRA | 2015, 2019 |
| GAB | 2020, 2023 |
| RWA | 2021-2023 |

    Get an overview of missing data in your data frame¶

    overview_na visualises missing values in your data. It returns a horizontal bar chart showing the share of NAs per variable (column-wise, the default) or per observation (row-wise). You can also augment the original data frame with the computed NA counts and percentages.

    Parameter Default Description
    yaxis "Variables" y-axis label; auto-set to "Observations" when row_wise=True
    perc True True = percentage of NAs, False = absolute counts
    row_wise False False = per column, True = per row
    add False when True (requires row_wise=True), returns the data frame extended with na_count and percentage instead of a plot
    In [8]:
    Copied! 
    ov_na = Overview(df=df_na, id_col='id', time='year')

# Default: column-wise, percentage
ov_na.overview_na()
    ov_na = Overview(df=df_na, id_col='id', time='year') # Default: column-wise, percentage ov_na.overview_na()
    Out[8]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Number of NA (in %)', ylabel='Variables'>
    In [9]:
    Copied! 
    # Absolute counts instead of percentage
ov_na.overview_na(perc=False)
    # Absolute counts instead of percentage ov_na.overview_na(perc=False)
    Out[9]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Number of NA (total)', ylabel='Variables'>
    In [10]:
    Copied! 
    # Custom y-axis label
ov_na.overview_na(yaxis="My Variables")
    # Custom y-axis label ov_na.overview_na(yaxis="My Variables")
    Out[10]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Number of NA (in %)', ylabel='My Variables'>
    In [11]:
    Copied! 
    # Row-wise: one bar per observation
ov_na.overview_na(row_wise=True)
    # Row-wise: one bar per observation ov_na.overview_na(row_wise=True)
    Out[11]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Number of NA (in %)', ylabel='Observations'>
    In [12]:
    Copied! 
    # Row-wise and augment the data frame with na_count and percentage columns
ov_na.overview_na(row_wise=True, add=True)
    # Row-wise and augment the data frame with na_count and percentage columns ov_na.overview_na(row_wise=True, add=True)
    Out[12]:
    id year na_count percentage
    0 RWA 2022.0 0 0.0
    1 RWA 2001.0 0 0.0
    2 RWA 2000.0 0 0.0
    3 NaN 2023.0 1 50.0
    4 GAB 2021.0 0 0.0
    5 GAB 2023.0 0 0.0
    6 FRA 2020.0 0 0.0
    7 FRA 2019.0 0 0.0
    8 BEL NaN 1 50.0
    9 BEL 2015.0 0 0.0
    10 ARG 2014.0 0 0.0
    11 NaN 2013.0 1 50.0
    12 NaN 2002.0 1 50.0

    Get a column-level summary of your data frame¶

    overview_summary returns a data frame with one row per column, showing:

    • non_null_count — number of non-missing values
    • unique_count — number of distinct non-missing values
    • sample_values — up to 5 example values

    It works on any data frame regardless of whether id and time are set.

    In [13]:
    Copied! 
    ov_na.overview_summary()
    ov_na.overview_summary()
    Out[13]:
    non_null_count unique_count sample_values
    column
    id 10 5 [RWA, GAB, FRA, BEL, ARG]
    year 12 11 [2022.0, 2001.0, 2000.0, 2023.0, 2021.0]

    Visualize observation coverage across id and time¶

    overview_plot creates a connected dot-plot showing which id–time combinations are present in the data:

    • each id is a row on the y-axis
    • time is on the x-axis
    • consecutive years are joined by a line; gaps produce separate point clusters

    This makes it easy to spot coverage gaps at a glance.

    In [14]:
    Copied! 
    # Basic coverage plot
overview.overview_plot()
    # Basic coverage plot overview.overview_plot()
    Out[14]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Time frame', ylabel='Sample'>

    Color-coding by a third variable¶

    Pass any column name to color to color-code each point by that variable. A legend is added automatically at the bottom of the plot.

    In [15]:
    Copied! 
    data_color = {
    'id':     ['RWA', 'RWA', 'RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'FRA', 'FRA', 'BEL', 'BEL', 'ARG'],
    'year':   [2022,  2023,  2021,  2023,  2020,  2019,  2015,  2014,  2013,  2002],
    'regime': ['Dem', 'Dem', 'Aut', 'Aut', 'Aut', 'Dem', 'Dem', 'Dem', 'Dem', 'Aut'],
}

df_color = pd.DataFrame(data_color)
ov_color = Overview(df=df_color, id_col='id', time='year')

ov_color.overview_plot(color='regime')
    data_color = { 'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', 'RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'FRA', 'FRA', 'BEL', 'BEL', 'ARG'], 'year': [2022, 2023, 2021, 2023, 2020, 2019, 2015, 2014, 2013, 2002], 'regime': ['Dem', 'Dem', 'Aut', 'Aut', 'Aut', 'Dem', 'Dem', 'Dem', 'Dem', 'Aut'], } df_color = pd.DataFrame(data_color) ov_color = Overview(df=df_color, id_col='id', time='year') ov_color.overview_plot(color='regime')
    Out[15]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Time frame', ylabel='Sample'>

    Additional parameters¶

    overview_plot exposes several parameters for fine-tuning the output:

    Parameter Default Description
    xaxis "Time frame" x-axis label
    yaxis "Sample" y-axis label
    asc True Sort ids top-to-bottom in ascending order
    color None Column name to color-code points by
    dot_size 2 Size of the plotted squares
    show_plot True Display the plot inline; set to False to return the axes object only
    In [16]:
    Copied! 
    # Custom axis labels and descending id order
overview.overview_plot(
    xaxis='Year',
    yaxis='Country',
    asc=False,
)
    # Custom axis labels and descending id order overview.overview_plot( xaxis='Year', yaxis='Country', asc=False, )
    Out[16]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Year', ylabel='Country'>

    Compare the overlap of two data frames¶

    overview_overlap compares the ID coverage of two data frames. Call it on an Overview instance and pass the second data frame as dat2.

    • plot_type="bar" (default) — grouped bar chart showing observation counts per identifier.
    • plot_type="venn" — Venn diagram showing the number of IDs unique to each data set and shared between both.
    Parameter Default Description
    dat2 — Second data frame to compare against
    dat2_id — Column name of the ID variable in dat2
    dat1_name "Data set 1" Label for this data set in the plot
    dat2_name "Data set 2" Label for dat2 in the plot
    plot_type "bar" "bar" for a grouped bar chart, "venn" for a Venn diagram
    show_plot True Display the plot inline
    In [17]:
    Copied! 
    # Generate a second data frame that partially overlaps with df
data2 = {
    'id':   ['RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'ARG', 'ARG'],
    'year': [2022, 2023, 2020, 2019, 2018]
}
df2 = pd.DataFrame(data2)

# Bar chart (default)
overview.overview_overlap(dat2=df2, dat2_id='id', dat1_name='Survey 1', dat2_name='Survey 2')
    # Generate a second data frame that partially overlaps with df data2 = { 'id': ['RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'ARG', 'ARG'], 'year': [2022, 2023, 2020, 2019, 2018] } df2 = pd.DataFrame(data2) # Bar chart (default) overview.overview_overlap(dat2=df2, dat2_id='id', dat1_name='Survey 1', dat2_name='Survey 2')
    Out[17]:
    <Axes: title={'center': 'Overlap of data sets'}, xlabel='Identifier', ylabel='Count (absolute number of observations)'>
    In [18]:
    Copied! 
    # Venn diagram
overview.overview_overlap(dat2=df2, dat2_id='id', dat1_name='Survey 1', dat2_name='Survey 2', plot_type='venn')
    # Venn diagram overview.overview_overlap(dat2=df2, dat2_id='id', dat1_name='Survey 1', dat2_name='Survey 2', plot_type='venn')
    Out[18]:
    <Axes: title={'center': 'Overlap of data sets'}>

    Visualize sample coverage as a heat map¶

    overview_heat plots a heat map that shows how many observations exist for each id-time combination. Set perc=True together with exp_total to display coverage as a percentage of the expected maximum.

    Parameter Default Description
    perc False False = absolute count per cell, True = percentage
    exp_total None Denominator for percentages; required when perc=True
    xaxis "Time frame" x-axis label
    yaxis "Sample" y-axis label
    col_low "#dceaf2" Hex color for the lowest value
    col_high "#2A5773" Hex color for the highest value
    label True Display values inside each cell
    show_plot True Display the plot inline
    In [19]:
    Copied! 
    overview.overview_heat()
    overview.overview_heat()
    Out[19]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Time frame', ylabel='Sample'>
    In [20]:
    Copied! 
    overview.overview_heat(perc=True, exp_total=3)
    overview.overview_heat(perc=True, exp_total=3)
    Out[20]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Time frame', ylabel='Sample'>

    Visualize two conditions against their thresholds¶

    overview_crossplot plots a scatter of two numeric conditions, drawing vertical and horizontal lines at user-defined thresholds to divide observations into four quadrants. Data are aggregated to (id, time) means before plotting, so duplicate rows are handled automatically.

    Parameter Default Description
    cond1 — Column name of the first condition (x-axis)
    cond2 — Column name of the second condition (y-axis)
    threshold1 — Threshold for cond1, drawn as a vertical line
    threshold2 — Threshold for cond2, drawn as a horizontal line
    xaxis "Condition 1" x-axis label
    yaxis "Condition 2" y-axis label
    label False Annotate each point with its id–time label
    color False Color points by quadrant
    dot_size 20 Marker size
    fontsize 8 Font size for point labels when label=True
    show_plot True Display the plot inline
    In [21]:
    Copied! 
    # Generate data with two numeric conditions
data_cross = {
    'id':         ['RWA', 'RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'FRA', 'FRA', 'BEL', 'ARG'],
    'year':       [2020,  2021,  2020,  2021,  2020,  2021,  2020,  2020],
    'gdp':        [8000,  9000,  22000, 24000, 40000, 42000, 35000, 12000],
    'population': [13e6,  13.5e6, 2.2e6, 2.3e6, 67e6, 68e6, 11e6,  45e6],
}
df_cross = pd.DataFrame(data_cross)

ovw_cross = Overview(df=df_cross, id_col='id', time='year')

# Basic crossplot: threshold lines divide the scatter into four quadrants
ovw_cross.overview_crossplot(
    cond1='gdp',
    cond2='population',
    threshold1=25000,
    threshold2=27e6,
)
    # Generate data with two numeric conditions data_cross = { 'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'FRA', 'FRA', 'BEL', 'ARG'], 'year': [2020, 2021, 2020, 2021, 2020, 2021, 2020, 2020], 'gdp': [8000, 9000, 22000, 24000, 40000, 42000, 35000, 12000], 'population': [13e6, 13.5e6, 2.2e6, 2.3e6, 67e6, 68e6, 11e6, 45e6], } df_cross = pd.DataFrame(data_cross) ovw_cross = Overview(df=df_cross, id_col='id', time='year') # Basic crossplot: threshold lines divide the scatter into four quadrants ovw_cross.overview_crossplot( cond1='gdp', cond2='population', threshold1=25000, threshold2=27e6, )
    Out[21]:
    <Axes: xlabel='Condition 1', ylabel='Condition 2'>
    In [22]:
    Copied! 
    # With quadrant coloring and point labels
ovw_cross.overview_crossplot(
    cond1='gdp',
    cond2='population',
    threshold1=25000,
    threshold2=27e6,
    color=True,
    label=True,
    xaxis='GDP per capita',
    yaxis='Population',
)
    # With quadrant coloring and point labels ovw_cross.overview_crossplot( cond1='gdp', cond2='population', threshold1=25000, threshold2=27e6, color=True, label=True, xaxis='GDP per capita', yaxis='Population', )
    Out[22]:
    <Axes: xlabel='GDP per capita', ylabel='Population'>

    Export an overview table as LaTeX¶

    overview_latex converts the result of overview_tab into a LaTeX table. By default it prints the LaTeX to the console. Pass save_out=True together with a file_path to write it directly to a .tex file instead.

    In [23]:
    Copied! 
    print(overview.overview_latex(df_overview, title="Time and scope of the sample", id_label="Country", time_label="Years"))
    print(overview.overview_latex(df_overview, title="Time and scope of the sample", id_label="Country", time_label="Years"))
    % Overview table generated in Python using overviewpy
% Table created on 2026-07-11
\begin{table}[ht]
 \centering
 \caption{Time and scope of the sample}
\label{tab:tab1}
\begin{tabular}{ll}
 \hline
Country & Years \\ \hline
ARG & 2002 \\
BEL & 2013-2014 \\
FRA & 2015, 2019 \\
GAB & 2020, 2023 \\
RWA & 2021-2023 \\
\hline
 \end{tabular}
 \end{table}

    Sort observations into a 2×2 cross table¶

    overview_crosstab divides a dataset into a 2×2 table based on two numeric conditions and their thresholds. Each cell lists the id–time combinations that satisfy (or do not satisfy) each condition. Duplicate (id, time) rows are averaged before thresholding.

    In [24]:
    Copied! 
    data_cross = {
    'id':  ['RWA', 'RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'FRA', 'FRA', 'BEL', 'BEL', 'ARG'],
    'year': [2020,  2021,  2020,  2021,  2020,  2021,  2020,  2021,  2020],
    'gdp':  [30000, 32000, 20000, 21000, 35000, 36000, 15000, 15500, 28000],
    'pop':  [13000000, 13500000, 2300000, 2400000, 67000000, 68000000,
             11500000, 11600000, 45000000],
}
df_cross = pd.DataFrame(data_cross)
ovw_cross = Overview(df=df_cross, id_col='id', time='year')

ovw_cross.overview_crosstab(
    cond1='gdp',
    cond2='pop',
    threshold1=25000,
    threshold2=10000000,
)
    data_cross = { 'id': ['RWA', 'RWA', 'GAB', 'GAB', 'FRA', 'FRA', 'BEL', 'BEL', 'ARG'], 'year': [2020, 2021, 2020, 2021, 2020, 2021, 2020, 2021, 2020], 'gdp': [30000, 32000, 20000, 21000, 35000, 36000, 15000, 15500, 28000], 'pop': [13000000, 13500000, 2300000, 2400000, 67000000, 68000000, 11500000, 11600000, 45000000], } df_cross = pd.DataFrame(data_cross) ovw_cross = Overview(df=df_cross, id_col='id', time='year') ovw_cross.overview_crosstab( cond1='gdp', cond2='pop', threshold1=25000, threshold2=10000000, )
    Out[24]:
    gdp >= 25000 gdp < 25000
    pop >= 10000000 ARG (2020), FRA (2020-2021), RWA (2020-2021) BEL (2020-2021)
    pop < 10000000 GAB (2020-2021)

    Cookie consent

    I took great care to disable all third party cookies and scripts. However, there might be some cookies left which would like to be used.
